title: Emergency Guide: What to Do if Your Dog Swallowed a Button Battery
meta_desc: Swallowing a button battery is a critical emergency for dogs. Learn the immediate steps to take and why every minute counts for your pet's safety.
content_html:
TL;DR: Button battery ingestion is a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent severe internal tissue damage. Do not attempt to induce vomiting, as this can cause further chemical burns to the esophagus.

What happens if my dog swallows a button battery?

Button battery ingestion occurs when a dog swallows a small, disc-shaped battery commonly found in watches, remote controls, or hearing aids. These objects are extremely hazardous because they contain caustic chemicals and can generate an electrical current when in contact with moist internal tissues.

Is button battery ingestion a veterinary emergency?

  • Contact a veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital immediately, as this is a high-urgency emergency.
  • Seek medical help even if the battery has not leaked, as it can cause severe tissue necrosis within two hours.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear before seeking professional help.
  • Do not attempt to induce vomiting, which could cause additional chemical burns to the throat.

How does a photo of the battery help the veterinarian triage my pet?

  • Take a quick photo of the packaging or an identical battery to show the medical team.
  • Identify the exact size, voltage, and chemistry (such as lithium vs. alkaline) to help determine the level of risk.
  • Provide information that helps the veterinarian decide between immediate surgical or endoscopic removal and close monitoring.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If a dog has swallowed a button battery, radiography should be performed to determine the location of the battery casing; however, be aware that some batteries do not show up well on radiographs. For intact batteries that have been swallowed without chewing, emesis may be induced to expel the battery unless there is a possibility the battery casing has been punctured. If the battery casing is suspected to be punctured, emesis should not be induced due to the risk of alkaline gel leakage onto oral and esophageal mucosa. If a disk battery has been ingested, administer 20 mL boluses of tap water every 15 minutes to decrease the severity and delay the development of current-induced esophageal ulceration. Batteries that do not pass through the pylorus within 48 hours are unlikely to pass on their own and may require surgical or endoscopic removal. Batteries that have obviously been punctured should be removed surgically to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration due to leakage of alkaline gel. Serial radiography to verify battery location is recommended until the battery is expelled.

Chapter: Toxicology, Gastroenterology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog anything to eat after they swallow a battery?
faqs.answer: Do not give your dog any food, water, or medication until you have spoken with a veterinarian, as this may complicate necessary sedation or surgery.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of battery damage in dogs?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include heavy drooling, oral pain, refusal to eat, vomiting, and abdominal sensitivity, though internal damage can occur before any outward signs are visible.

faqs.question: Will the battery pass through my dog naturally?
faqs.answer: While some small batteries may pass, the risk of electrical burns or leaking acid is too high to 'wait and see.' A veterinarian must use X-rays to locate the battery and assess the risk.
